sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 53-year-old female, presents with a 3-month history of chest pain that occurs when walking and resolves when resting.
She describes the pain as dull and pressure-like, without radiation or exacerbation by any specific activities.
She denies any shortness of breath, sweating, or clamminess.
The patient has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure, with a previous hemoglobin A1c of 7.0% and a family history of heart disease.
She is currently taking metformin 500mg twice daily.
She reports being addicted to diet soda and has been unable to exercise due to the chest pain.
She has an upcoming appointment with ophthalmology in two weeks for diabetic retinopathy.
Review of Systems:
The patient denies any nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea related to her diabetes or medications.
She has noticed a trend of high blood pressure in her chart, but has not been diagnosed or treated for it yet.
Current Medications:
Metformin 500mg twice daily
Objective:
Vital Signs:
Blood pressure: Not mentioned
Pulse rate: Not mentioned
Oxygen saturation: Not mentioned
Temperature: Not mentioned
Physical Exam:
The patient's exam is normal except for 1+ non-pitting edema in the lower legs and ankles.
The heart exam reveals a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
The lung and abdominal exams are normal.
Test Results:
EKG: Normal, no signs of a heart attack
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a 3-month history of chest pain that may be related to her suspected high blood pressure and diabetes.
Problem List: Chest pain; Suspected high blood pressure; Diabetes mellitus; Unstable angina; Systolic ejection murmur; Lower extremity edema.
Plan:
I started the patient on aspirin 81mg daily for chest pain.
I also prescribed nitroglycerin for chest pain, with instructions to take up to 3 pills every 5 minutes if the pain does not resolve.
I will refer the patient to cardiology for further evaluation and a stress test.
I will continue the patient on metformin 500mg twice daily and will order another hemoglobin A1c in 2 weeks.
I will also start the patient on hydrochlorothiazide for high blood pressure and instruct her to use a blood pressure cuff at home and enter her readings in her chart.
I will see the patient in 2 weeks to monitor her blood pressure and make any necessary adjustments.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen in 2 weeks to follow up on her chest pain, diabetes, and high blood pressure.
